one_line_takeaway: A CAN-bus VIDS survey that goes further than prior surveys by reproducing ~18 intrusion detectors on one self-collected real-CAN (2014 Toyota Corolla) attack dataset, empirically showing that periodicity-based detectors collapse on masquerade and diagnostic attacks while no single method covers the full attack spectrum.
key_finding: Existing VIDS are narrow: each concentrates on a subset of attacks and no method covers the full spectrum. Frequency-, entropy-, ID-sequence-, and similarity-based detectors depend on CAN message periodicity (ID stability), so they detect injection/flooding well but FAIL on masquerade attacks (SPA-3) that preserve normal timing/ID cadence, and fail on diagnostic-message attacks whose traffic is non-periodic. Concretely, Gmiden et al.'s frequency method scored precision=recall=F1=0 on SPA-3, and 0 on DIA-1/2/3/4 despite accuracy=1 (it cannot separate normal from malicious diagnostic messages); Muter et al.'s relative- and overall-entropy methods both miss SPA-1 and SPA-3, and the overall-entropy variant additionally misses FUA-2. Payload-based methods can catch masquerade but suffer low detection rates because a stable model of normal CAN payloads is hard to build. High accuracy alone is misleading given class imbalance (malicious << normal in every dataset).
core_contribution: A comprehensive survey AND a hands-on comparative evaluation of Vehicular Intrusion Detection Systems (VIDS) for the automotive CAN bus. The authors' own stated four contributions are: (1) they analyzed 34 vehicle-attack research studies and systematically classified them into 18 distinct attack types under a 5-family taxonomy (Sniffing/SNA, Spoofing/SPA, DoS/DOA, Fuzzing/FUA, Diagnostic/DIA); (2) they examined 53 different VIDS, comparing their threat models, defense scenarios, and detection mechanisms (Table IV classifies them by data feature, e.g. ECU fingerprint vs. semantic/parameter-based); (3) they REPRODUCED ~17-19 parameter-monitoring VIDS and evaluated them head-to-head on a single unified dataset the authors collected themselves from a real CAN bus; and (4) they distill practicality limitations and future trends (CAN-FD/CAN-XL, Automotive Ethernet, J1939 heavy-duty CAN, and Internet-of-Vehicles IDS). The reproduction-under-a-common-dataset is the element that distinguishes this from the ~13 prior VIDS surveys it critiques (Table I), all of which the authors note perform no reimplementation.
threat_model: The paper consolidates the threat models of the surveyed VIDS into three dimensions (Fig. 2). (a) Access surfaces: physical (OBD-II dongle, brief device insertion, ECU firmware tampering during repair) vs. network/remote (Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, cellular, TPMS/FM/RDS radio channels). (b) Attack capabilities, four tiers: inserting a malicious OBD device, partially compromising an ECU (listen-only/suspend, e.g. via MCP2515 SPI mode or diagnostic session change), fully compromising an ECU (arbitrary message injection), and inserting an external ECU. (c) Attack purposes: remotely controlling the vehicle, malfunctioning/losing control (fuzzing, bus-off DoS), and spoofing diagnostic tools to hide missing safety components. Root cause throughout: CAN's broadcast nature, lack of authentication/encryption, no segmentation, and an exploitable arbitration/error-handling mechanism.
limitations: As a critique of the paper itself: (1) the reproduction results are presented only as per-attack bar charts (Fig. 6) with no exact numeric table, so precise per-method/per-attack scores are not recoverable from the text beyond the zero-detection cases discussed. (2) The unified dataset comes from a single, older vehicle (2014 Toyota Corolla, only 3 ECUs / 23 IDs), limiting generalizability to modern multi-domain IVNs. (3) The evaluated systems are the authors' own reimplementations with self-chosen parameters, which may not match original authors' tuned implementations, and the authors themselves caution that cross-method comparison is confounded by divergent threat models. (4) Fingerprint- and voltage-based VIDS (arguably the state of the art) are omitted from the empirical comparison due to data-collection constraints. (5) It is a survey/evaluation, not a new detector: no novel defense is proposed, only recommendations.

datasets_used: Authors' self-collected real-CAN dataset from a 2014 Toyota Corolla testbed (real ECUs + network; 3 internal ECUs, 23 distinct CAN IDs), tapped at CAN-H/CAN-L

11 derived labeled attack datasets (Table V): SPA-1 replay, SPA-2 fabrication, SPA-3 masquerade, DOA-1 high-priority DoS, DOA-2 redundant-injection DoS, FUA-1 disorderly-control fuzzing, FUA-2 reverse fuzzing, DIA-2 diagnostic control, DIA-3 diagnostic spoof, DIA-4 diagnostic fuzz, DIA-Normal (normal diagnostic messages, 0 malicious)
quantitative_results: Survey scope: 34 attack studies classified into 18 attack types; 53 VIDS examined; ~17-19 parameter-monitoring VIDS reproduced on the unified dataset
Gmiden et al. frequency-based method: precision=recall=F1=0 on SPA-3 masquerade; on DIA-1..DIA-4 accuracy=1 but precision=recall=F1=0 (cannot distinguish normal vs malicious diagnostic traffic)
Muter et al. relative-entropy method fails to detect SPA-1 and SPA-3; overall-entropy variant additionally fails on FUA-2 and detects diagnostic attacks poorly

Context figures cited from the automotive-cybersecurity market and CAN: market projected to USD 3574.5M by 2028 from USD 571M in 2021 (CAGR 29.6%); modern vehicles have 70-100 ECUs; CAN max 1 Mbps, CAN-FD up to 8 Mbps, ISO 11898-2:2024 raises CAN to 20 Mbps / 2048-byte payloads
Prior-survey comparison (Table I) covered 4-40 works each and none performed reimplementation/experiments
baselines_compared: Reproduced VIDS evaluated in Fig. 6 (the paper's own experiment): Gmiden et al., Song et al., Moore et al., Young et al., Olufowobi et al., Marchetti et al. (two works), Muter et al. (relative-entropy and overall-entropy variants), Tomlinson et al. (Z-score and ARIMA variants), Wu et al., Ohira et al., Taylor et al. (two works), Kang et al., Stabili et al.

Prior VIDS surveys critiqued in Table I (Related Work, NOT this paper's results): Rajapaksha et al. 2023 (40), Karopoulos et al. 2022 (40), Aliwa et al. 2021 (30), Xie et al. 2021 (23), Hafeez et al. 2020 (5), Wu et al. 2019 (20), Young et al. 2019 (15), Al et al. 2019 (24), Lokman et al. 2019 (25), Dupont et al. 2019 (24), Tomlinson et al. 2018 (17), Avatefipour et al. 2018 (5), Liu et al. 2017 (4)
